nozzles: Mounted deck gun smooth bore tip
sceneElements.elevation: Mounted deck gun; no elevation add-on for deck-gun height

problems.title: Deck Gun Flow
problems.question: Engine 181 is operating a deck gun with a 1¾-inch smooth bore tip at 80 PSI nozzle pressure. Use the smooth-bore flow formula: GPM = 29.7 x diameter^2 x √NP. What is the approximate total deck gun flow?
problems.instructorExplanation: Use the smooth-bore flow formula for a 1¾-inch tip at 80 PSI nozzle pressure. The result is approximately 815 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: This is a flow question. Do not enter pump discharge pressure; calculate the GPM from the tip size and nozzle pressure.

problems.title: Pump Discharge Pressure
problems.question: Engine 181 is operating a deck gun mounted directly on the apparatus with a 1¾-inch smooth bore tip flowing approximately 815 GPM at 80 PSI nozzle pressure. Because this is a mounted deck gun, treat it as part of the fire truck: do not add elevation for deck-gun height. There is no hose friction loss and no appliance loss. What pump discharge pressure should be set?
formulaBreakdown: Mounted deck gun = part of the apparatus
Deck-gun height is not added as elevation pressure
PDP = NP + FL + elevation + appliance loss
problems.instructorExplanation: Do not add elevation pressure for the height of a truck-mounted deck gun. With no discharge hose friction loss and no appliance loss, pump discharge pressure equals nozzle pressure: 80 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: If you entered 95 PSI, you added elevation for the deck gun height. Mounted deck guns are treated as part of the apparatus, so elevation is 0 PSI.

problems.title: Alternate Deck Gun Tip Flow
problems.question: Engine 181 switches to a 1½-inch smooth bore deck gun tip operating at 80 PSI nozzle pressure. Use the smooth-bore flow formula: GPM = 29.7 x diameter^2 x √NP. What is the approximate flow?
problems.instructorExplanation: A 1½-inch smooth bore tip at 80 PSI flows about 600 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Use the smooth-bore flow formula with the changed tip size.

problems.title: Hydrant Pressure Drop
problems.question: Engine 181 is supplying one deck gun flowing 815 GPM. Hydrant static pressure is 80 PSI and residual pressure is 65 PSI. Use the hydrant flow chart: pressure drop % = (static - residual) / static x 100. Chart: 0-10% drop = 3 more equivalent lines, 10-15% = 2, 15-25% = 1, over 25% = 0. How many additional equivalent 815 GPM deck gun streams could likely be supplied?
problems.instructorExplanation: The pressure drop is about 19%, which falls in the 15-25% range on the chart. That indicates about one additional equivalent stream.
problems.explainMistake: Do not subtract from the 20 PSI minimum here; use the provided hydrant pressure-drop chart.

problems.title: Additional Attack Line Flow
problems.question: The deck gun is flowing 815 GPM. If one additional 185 GPM attack line is placed in service, what is the new total flow requirement?
formulaBreakdown: Total flow = deck gun flow + attack line flow
problems.instructorExplanation: Add the deck gun flow and the attack line flow together for the total water demand: 1000 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: This is a total-flow question. Add both flowing devices.
